Moses’ patience was tested not only by the people but also within his own family. The claim was that he had married a foreign wife; however, it is likely their pride was hurt and their envy stirred up by his greater authority. Opposition from close relatives and religious friends is very painful. But this is to be expected, and it will be beneficial if we can maintain Moses’ gentleness and meekness in such circumstances. Moses was thus prepared for the work he was called to do. God not only cleared Moses but praised him. Moses had the spirit of prophecy in a way that set him far above all other prophets; yet whoever is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he, and our Lord Jesus infinitely surpasses him. Miriam and Aaron should consider whom they insulted. We have reason to be afraid of saying or doing anything against God’s servants. Those who are not afraid to speak evil of authorities are truly presumptuous. The removal of God's presence is the surest and saddest sign of God's displeasure. Woe to us if he departs; he never departs until we drive him away through sin and foolishness.

The cloud departed, and Miriam became leprous. When God goes, evil comes: do not expect good when God departs. Her foul tongue, as Bishop Hall says, was justly punished with a disfigured face. Aaron, as priest, judged the leprosy. He could not pronounce her leprous without trembling, knowing himself to be equally guilty. But if she was thus punished for speaking against Moses, what will become of those who sin against Christ? Aaron, who joined his sister in speaking against Moses, was forced, for himself and his sister, to beseech him and to speak highly of the one he had so recently blamed. Those who trample upon God’s saints and servants will one day be glad to seek their favor. It is beneficial when rebukes lead to confession of sin and repentance. Such offenders, though corrected and disgraced, will be pardoned. Moses showed that he forgave the injury done to him. We must follow this example of Moses, and also the example of our Savior, who said, “Father, forgive them.” A reason is given for Miriam being put out of the camp for seven days: because she should accept her punishment in this way. When we experience the signs of God’s displeasure due to sin, it becomes us to take shame to ourselves. This hindered the people's progress in their march toward Canaan. Many things oppose us, but nothing so hinders us on the path to heaven as sin.
